nativeRange.habitat: Cool, well-oxygenated freshwater streams and rivers with leaf litter, rocks, and submerged wood; the wild form is the striped 'Tiger' variant from which captive blue, orange-eye, red, and black morphs have been line-bred.

decorPreferences: Dense moss (Java, Christmas, Flame) for cover and grazing surface
Indian almond leaves and other botanicals to seed biofilm
Driftwood and rock crevices for hiding during moults
Inert or mildly buffering substrate (Blue Tigers tolerate inert better than Crystals)
Sponge or pre-filter to prevent shrimplets from being drawn in

diet.notes: Generally treated as a biofilm-and-algae grazer that also accepts shrimp pellets, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, carrot), and the occasional protein supplement (frozen daphnia, snowflake food). Overfeeding has been widely reported as the leading cause of dwarf-shrimp losses — small daily portions consumed within a couple of hours are commonly advised.

goodWith: Other peaceful Caridina morphs that share parameters (Crystal Red/Black, other Tiger lines)
Small peaceful fish unlikely to predate shrimplets (Otocinclus, small Pygmy Corydoras)
Live planted setups with moss and botanicals
avoidWith: Most community fish that will eat shrimplets (tetras, barbs, gouramis, rainbowfish, most catfish)

Neocaridina (Cherry/Blue Dream/Yellow) if pure-strain breeding is desired — hybridisation is not possible between genera, but inter-Caridina morphs in the same tank will produce off-coloured offspring
Copper-based medications and plant fertilisers (lethal to all dwarf shrimp)

summary: The Blue Tiger Shrimp is a selectively bred colour morph of the Tiger Shrimp (Caridina mariae), appreciated for its vivid blue patterning and relative hardiness among Caridina shrimp. It does best in soft, cool, stable freshwater and is available in several distinct sub-morphs, including the Orange-Eye Blue Tiger, Royal Blue Tiger, Black Tiger, and Red Tiger.
careNotes: The Tiger Shrimp lineage carries a taxonomically debated history — long sold under the name Caridina cf. cantonensis 'Tiger', it is now generally placed under Caridina mariae following molecular work that identified C. mariae as the sister species to C. cantonensis. Temperature is generally advised to stay between 19 and 24°C; sustained warmer water has been reported to shorten lifespan and reduce egg viability. Unlike most other Caridina, Blue Tigers are broadly considered to do well on inert substrate and do not strictly require active-buffering soil. Several colour sub-morphs are sold under the umbrella name: Orange-Eye Blue Tiger, Royal Blue Tiger (a deeper, almost solid-blue morph with orange eyes, developed as a cross between Orange-Eye Royal Blue and Black Tiger lines), Black Tiger, Red Tiger, and Super Tiger (noted for higher-grade striping). These sub-morphs do not breed true; offspring from a mixed colony will vary in shade and stripe intensity. Blue Tigers are generally considered sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and copper — a fully cycled, mature tank with stable parameters and no copper-bearing medications or fertilisers is widely advised. A minimum tank size of around 40 L (10 US gal) is typically recommended for a small starter colony, though larger volumes buffer parameter swings more effectively.
breedingNotes: Blue Tiger Shrimp are generally regarded as easy-to-moderate to breed once water parameters are stable. Berried females carry 20–40 eggs tucked under their abdomen and fan them continuously with their small swimming legs (pleopods) to keep the eggs oxygenated. Expected hatching time is roughly 28 days at around 22°C. Shrimplets emerge as fully formed miniatures rather than passing through a larval stage, and they begin grazing biofilm immediately — a mature, biofilm-rich tank is considered essential for raising them successfully. Because Tigers and Crystals belong to the same genus, the two will hybridise if kept together, producing intermediate offspring sometimes called 'Tibee' shrimp. Hobbyists who want to maintain a pure phenotype are generally advised to keep Tiger colonies separate from Crystal lines.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
primary.notes: Primary swap: The Shrimp Farm (original primary) returned HTTP 403 on both the root and several deep-linked caresheet URLs; Aquarium Breeder's dedicated Blue Tiger guide was used as the primary in its place. Provided scientific name (C. mariae, formerly C. cf. cantonensis 'Blue Tiger'), water parameters (T 19–24°C, pH 7.2–7.4 ideal / 6.5–8.0 tolerated, GH 6–8, KH 2–4, TDS 180–220), size 2.5–3 cm, lifespan 1–2 yr, tank ~40 L, breeding easy–moderate, peaceful temperament, and the colour-morph list.

additional.name: Flip Aquatics — Royal Blue Tiger product page
additional.notes: Secondary as designated by manifest. Confirms Royal Blue Tiger is a cultivated cross (OE Royal Blue × Black Tiger), commercial water-parameter targets (T 68–74°F / 20–23°C, pH 7.1–7.6, GH 7–10, KH 2–4, TDS 150–210) — closely aligned with primary. Provides the deep-blue / orange-eye / no-stripe phenotype description.

additional.name: Rare Shrimp — Caridina mariae Tiger Shrimp
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Atyidae, order Decapoda), confirmed C. mariae assignment, adult size 2.5–3.8 cm, lifespan 1.5–2 yr, native range China/Taiwan freshwater streams, hatching 3–4 weeks. Their pH range (5.4–7.5) is wider/lower than the primary — averaged into the final pH range.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Caridina Shrimp
additional.notes: Genus-level secondary. Confirms Caridina temperature 20–24°C, pH 6–8, size 1–4.5 cm, lifespan 1–2 yr, and notes specifically that Blue Tigers are an outlier that 'prefers a pH below 7' — used as evidence that the morph tolerates the lower half of the Caridina pH band well.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caridina cantonensis
additional.notes: Taxonomic context only: cites Chen, Chen & Guo (2020) placing C. mariae as sister to C. cantonensis, supporting the modern reassignment of the Tiger lineage to C. mariae. Also confirmed hatching time ~28 days at 22°C and that colour morphs are selectively bred from the same species complex.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally moults every 3–6 weeks in healthy adults; juveniles moult more frequently. The discarded exoskeleton is usually left in the tank as a calcium source — has been reported to be re-consumed by the shrimp. A sudden cluster of failed moults (white-ring of death) is commonly attributed to mineral imbalance, especially GH or calcium too low, or to a copper spike.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Eggs hatch in roughly 28 days at around 22°C. Shrimplets emerge as fully formed miniatures with no larval stage and begin grazing biofilm immediately.
